title: Court Gravity — narration cues
notes: Section start/end times are derived from Manim play/wait durations in full_explainer.py,
then coda end is padded to match the rendered draft (245s).
spoken is the VO that should fit each window; full_text is the long-form essay from NARRATION.md.
beats are optional visual sync points relative to section start (seconds).

sections.title: Cold open
sections.spoken: Most analysis treats the court as flat. To a defense, it is not. Players pull attention differently. Defenders reshape that space the other way. What if we could see that invisible geometry?
sections.full_text: Most basketball analysis treats the court as flat. But to a defense, it is not. Every offensive player pulls attention toward a different part of the floor. Some stretch the defense beyond the three-point line. Some compress it toward the rim. Some make help arrive half a second earlier simply because the cost of being late is so high. Defenders reshape the same space in the opposite direction. They remove angles, discourage passes, and close opportunities before the box score ever records them. What if we could see that invisible geometry?

beats.note: player appears; well begins to dig

sections.title: 01 — The kernel
sections.spoken: One player. One field: location for center, strength for depth, sigma for reach. The blue well is offensive pressure.
sections.full_text: Begin with one offensive player. We represent his influence with a smooth Gaussian field. His location sets the center of the field. His strength determines its depth. Sigma controls how far that influence reaches. Close to the player, the value is strongest. Move away, and it falls smoothly rather than ending at an arbitrary boundary. The blue well is not shot probability by itself, and it is not claiming that the player physically bends the floor. It represents offensive pressure: space the defense must account for. That distinction matters. Gravity is valuable because of the decisions it forces, including the decisions that never appear in a traditional stat line.

beats.note: chapter card + equation grow from the opening field
center, depth, and reach highlight in sequence

sections.title: 02 — Strength and reach
sections.spoken: Same spot, different bend. Raise mass, and ignoring that player gets expensive. Raise sigma, and help farther away feels it. A high-gravity creator changes the whole help scheme.
sections.full_text: Two players can stand in the same location and bend the court differently. Increase mass and the well becomes deeper. In basketball language, ignoring that player becomes more expensive. Increase sigma and the field becomes wider. The player now affects help defenders who are farther away. Production, efficiency, shooting, and playmaking establish the strength of the field. Role and range help establish its radius. A high-gravity creator does not only occupy his own defender. He changes the shape of the entire help scheme.

sections.title: 03 — Two views
sections.spoken: This surface and the top-down heatmap are not different measurements. They are two views of the same function. In the angled view, valleys are offensive pull and ridges are defensive pressure. Overhead, equal-height contours become weather-map bands. Switch between them and the mental model stays intact.
sections.full_text: This surface and the top-down heatmap are not different measurements. They are two views of the same function. In the angled view, height makes the topology intuitive: valleys represent offensive pull, ridges represent defensive pressure. Rotate overhead and equal-height contours become the familiar weather-map bands. The two-dimensional view is better for locating space. The three-dimensional view is better for explaining why that space exists. Moving between them lets an audience preserve the same mental model.

beats.note: rotate to top-down weather map + contour scan

sections.title: 04 — Player ratings
sections.spoken: The shape begins with a player profile. For Luka, volume, efficiency, and playmaking build a broad offensive baseline. For Wembanyama, rim protection and mobility produce a strong defensive field. Ratings set the baseline. The live field then mixes that baseline with role, location, spacing, and the phase of play.
sections.full_text: The shape begins with a player profile. For Luka Dončić, scoring volume, efficiency, playmaking, and rebounding create a high offensive baseline with a broad area of influence. For Victor Wembanyama, rim protection, blocks, mobility, and defensive impact produce an unusually strong defensive field. These ratings do not decide the possession by themselves. They establish the baseline. The live field then combines that baseline with role, location, movement, teammate spacing, defender distance, and the current phase of play. In other words: who the player is matters, but where everyone is right now matters just as much.

beats.note: rating cards collapse into the live Luka–Wemby field

sections.title: 05 — Defensive cancellation
sections.spoken: Defense pushes back. Blue wells and red ridges meet everywhere. Close and balanced, they cancel — contested. Drift the defender away, and the offensive basin returns. Opportunity changes continuously.
sections.full_text: Defense contributes an opposing field. Blue offensive wells and red defensive ridges are compared at every point on the floor. When a defender is close, balanced, and positioned between the attacker and the scoring area, the two influences cancel. The surface returns toward neutral. A viewer should immediately read that area as contested. Now move the defender away. The red contribution weakens locally, and the offensive basin gradually reappears. There is no arbitrary open-or-closed switch. Opportunity changes continuously with separation, angle, and defensive quality.

beats.note: defender trail closes — signed meter approaches zero
defender drifts — signed meter and blue basin return

sections.title: 06 — Superposition
sections.spoken: One matchup teaches the idea. Basketball is never one matchup. First, five offensive wells. Then five defensive ridges — a connected help system. Finally, superpose both layers into one net surface: which side owns each region right now. That surface can reveal a pocket even when no single defender made an obvious mistake.
sections.full_text: One matchup is useful for understanding the idea, but basketball is never one matchup. First, add the five offensive fields. Each player contributes a different well based on his role and position. Then isolate the five defensive fields. Help responsibilities create a connected system of ridges rather than five independent circles. Finally, superpose both layers. The result is one net surface describing which side owns each region of the court at this instant. That surface can reveal a valuable pocket even when no single defender has made an obvious mistake.

beats.note: both contribution charts resolve into net advantage

sections.title: 07 — Spacing
sections.spoken: Separation alone is not enough. Crowd two threats and the defense covers both. Space the teammate weak-side, and gravity becomes useful. Look for a sustainable basin.
sections.full_text: Defender separation alone is not enough. If two offensive players occupy the same pocket, they do not create twice the value. Their fields overlap, the defense can guard two threats with one position, and the usable floor shrinks. Move the teammate to the opposite side. The defense must now cover more width, the floor regains balance, and the original player's gravity becomes useful. The opportunity signal becomes strongest when the defender is late and the other four players preserve playable spacing. That is the bright blue basin we are looking for: not merely an unguarded player, but a sustainable offensive advantage.

beats.note: movement paths widen spacing and clearance chart

sections.title: 08 — Game state
sections.spoken: The field must follow the state of the game. After a make, the topology disappears. On the inbound it rebuilds — through transition, the set, and the action. Continuous interpolation keeps pressure honest, so a dead-ball alignment is never mistaken for a live opportunity.
sections.full_text: The field must also follow the state of the game. After a made basket, the topology disappears because there is no live offensive pressure yet. During the inbound, the field begins at zero. It then rebuilds through transition, the set, and the action. The interpolation is continuous, so the visualization never pops from one shape to another. This keeps the model faithful to when pressure actually exists and prevents a dead-ball alignment from being mistaken for a live opportunity.

sections.title: 09 — Live possession
sections.spoken: Now watch the geometry, not only the ball. The handler turns the corner. Defense compresses. Weak side inherits two jobs. Help takes one more step, and the blue basin forms before the pass. The kickout reveals space the possession already shaped.
sections.full_text: Now watch the geometry instead of only the ball. The ball handler turns the corner and drives. Defenders compress toward the paint. The strong-side ridge becomes crowded while the weak-side defender inherits two responsibilities. The shooter stays spaced. His defender takes one more step toward the drive. The blue basin begins to form before the pass is thrown. The kickout does not create the opening at the last moment. It reveals space that the possession had already shaped. That is the key analytical idea: opportunity is often the accumulated result of several movements, not a single final action.

beats.note: kickout — pass line, open-space rings, and camera flatten together

sections.title: 10 — Counterfactual
sections.spoken: Because the model is continuous, ask a better question. What if the defender rotated sooner? Keep nine players fixed. Move only that defender. The ridge arrives, the basin flattens, the open look disappears. Restore the observed rotation, and opportunity returns. Replay becomes a teaching tool.
sections.full_text: Because the model is continuous, we can ask a more useful question. What if the defender had rotated sooner? Keep the other nine players fixed and move only that defender. The red ridge now reaches the shooter in time. The blue basin flattens, and the open look disappears. Restore the observed rotation and the opportunity returns. This counterfactual view turns the visualization from a replay graphic into a teaching tool. Coaches can compare responsibilities. Analysts can explain which movement created the advantage. Executives can see the result without decoding a spreadsheet.

beats.note: dashed alternate rotation path — observed state remains as ghost

sections.title: Coda
sections.spoken: The box score records what happened. Court Gravity shows why space opened, who created it, and what defense could change. A pedagogical model — not the proprietary NBA Gravity metric.
sections.full_text: The box score records what happened. Court Gravity helps explain why space opened, which player created it, and what the defense could have changed. This is a pedagogical influence model, not the proprietary NBA Gravity metric.

beats.note: closing cards over a dimmed, slowly moving field
